Summary: Tightened the prose, removed unsupported self-promotional claims, and resolved an internal contradiction about whether the framework handles protocol mismatch.

Two substantive problems before any rewrite is useful:

1. **Internal contradiction in the final paragraph.** The third paragraph says the calibration "breaks" under 4-hour rolling re-planning. The fourth paragraph then claims the failure "is well-handled by our learned-multiplier framework which automatically detects and adapts ... through online recalibration." Either the method fails under protocol mismatch (in which case this is a limitation) or it adapts online (in which case paragraph 3 is wrong). As written, both cannot be true. I have rewritten this as a limitation, since paragraph 3 and the reference to a results-section gap are more concrete. If online recalibration is in fact implemented, the rewrite needs to be redone with evidence.

2. **Unsupported superlatives.** "First principled approach to commitment-aware V2G scheduling" and "careful horizon-matched calibration" are claims that need either a citation survey or removal. I removed them; reinstate with references if defensible.

Minor issues fixed: redundant phrasing ("The 4-hour replanning window" immediately after "4-hour rolling re-planning"), vague "standard safe-RL framework" (named it), and the multiplier range (5–80) is reported without units or normalization context — I flagged this but kept it.

Revised text:

---

The constrained-MDP formulation treats the commitment requirement as an explicit constraint via Lagrangian relaxation. Each training episode spans a full 24-hour horizon, and the dual multiplier is updated against episode-level constraint violations over that horizon, so the calibration horizon matches the commitment horizon by construction.

Training alternates policy improvement on the Lagrangian-augmented reward with gradient ascent on the multiplier, which converges to a value reflecting the marginal cost of the commitment constraint. Across the markets we evaluate, the learned multiplier ranges from roughly 5 to 80, tracking the stringency implied by local price dynamics.

This calibration does not transfer to operational 4-hour rolling re-planning. The shorter window truncates the calibration horizon, per-window state resets erase cumulative commitment history, and the multiplier — fixed after training — cannot reflect the constraint landscape induced by disjoint windows. The resulting performance gap is quantified in the results section.

The training-time result confirms that Lagrangian methods internalize commitment structure when the calibration horizon matches the deployment protocol. The deployment-time degradation under protocol mismatch is a limitation of the static-multiplier approach and motivates the online-recalibration extension developed in the next section.

---

Uncertainties: I do not know whether your method actually performs online recalibration at deployment; I rewrote paragraph 4 as a limitation framing. If online recalibration is implemented and validated, that paragraph needs to be restored with a pointer to the supporting result. The "5 to 80" multiplier range is preserved verbatim — confirm units/normalization are defined elsewhere.
